1: On the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho, YHWH speaks to Moses concerning the Levites. The Levites receive no tribal territory (ch18:20-24 established this) but must live somewhere. Their provision is the subject of this chapter.
2: 'Command the Israelites to give the Levites from their hereditary possession cities to live in, and pasture lands (migrashim) around the cities for their livestock.' Migrash (pasture land) describes the ring of open ground around each city — a buffer between city and field. The Levites are given cities scattered throughout all tribal territories.
3: The cities shall be for the Levites to live in, and the pastureland for their livestock, their possessions, and all their animals. The Levites' provision is practical and complete — housing and land for their herds, drawn from each tribe's own inheritance. The priesthood is embedded in the community, not isolated from it.
4: The pastureland around the cities shall extend one thousand cubits in each direction from the city wall. The standardized measurement ensures every Levitical city has identical surrounding pasture, regardless of which tribe hosts them — equality of provision across the land.
5: Two thousand cubits are measured on each side — east, south, west, and north — with the city in the center. The city sits at the center of a 4,000-cubit square of pastureland. Precision here matters: the Levites' portion is carefully measured, not approximate. God's provision is exact.
6: Six of the forty-eight Levitical cities shall be cities of refuge (arei miklat), where the manslayer may flee. The cities of refuge are Levitical — administered by those who mediate between God and people. This is not coincidental: those who serve at the altar also serve as mediators of justice and mercy for those who shed blood accidentally.
7: Forty-eight cities in total shall be given to the Levites. Forty-eight from a nation of twelve tribes means four cities per tribe on average. The Levites are distributed throughout Israel — present in every region, serving every part of the people. Numbers 35:7 realizes the 'scattered in Israel' dimension of Jacob's prophecy about Simeon and Levi (Gen 49:7), but transformed from curse to vocation.
8: The cities shall be given from the possessions of the Israelites: from the larger tribes, more; from the smaller, fewer. Each gives proportionally. The principle of proportional giving — from whom much is given, much is required — is embedded in the land distribution itself.
9: YHWH speaks: 'Speak to the Israelites and say to them: When you cross the Jordan into Canaan...' The cities of refuge regulation begins with the crossing as the reference point. The institution is for life in the land — it presupposes settlement, community, and the possibility of accidental death among neighbors.
10: '...you shall designate cities to be cities of refuge for you.' Miklat (refuge/asylum) derives from a root meaning to receive or gather in. The city 'receives' the person fleeing. The image is of a place that takes in the otherwise condemned — a sanctuary.
11: Three cities east of the Jordan and three west, giving six total. The arrangement ensures no one in the land is more than a day's journey from a city of refuge. The institution was designed for accessibility — mercy should be reachable before the blood avenger (goel haddam) catches up.
12: These cities are a refuge from the blood avenger (goel haddam), so the manslayer shall not die until he stands before the congregation (edah) for trial. Goel haddam — literally 'redeemer of the blood' — is the nearest kinsman whose duty is to avenge a killed relative. The same word goel describes the kinsman-redeemer of Ruth. Justice and mercy share the same vocabulary.
13: Six cities of refuge for all Israel, giving access across the whole land. The repetition of 'six' and their placement east and west (v14-15) underscores that the institution is universal — not just for one tribe or region but for any person anywhere in Israel's territory.
14: Three cities across the Jordan and three in Canaan — these shall be cities of refuge. Moses himself designates the three east-Jordan cities before his death (Deut 4:41-43). Joshua designates the three west-Jordan cities (Josh 20:7-8). Two leaders, one institution — its establishment spans the transition of leadership.
15: 'These six cities shall be a refuge for the Israelites, for the resident alien, and for the sojourner among them.' The cities of refuge are not for Israelites only — ger (sojourner, resident alien) and toshav (temporary dweller) are also covered. Mercy is not ethnically restricted; the institution extends to all who live under Israel's covenant order.
16: But if someone strikes with an iron instrument and the victim dies, it is murder (ratzach); the murderer shall be put to death. The distinction between murder and manslaughter begins here with intent inferred from weapon: iron instruments were tools of deliberate killing, not accidental death.
17: Striking with a stone that can kill, if death results — this is murder; the murderer shall be put to death. The blood avenger shall put the murderer to death when he meets him. The law requires death for intentional killing; it does not leave vengeance to individual passion alone but channels it through defined roles.
18: Likewise if struck with a wooden weapon capable of killing — it is murder; the murderer shall be put to death. The progression through iron, stone, and wood exhausts the categories of hand weapons. Intent, not merely the act, determines guilt — but intent is inferred from the capacity of the weapon used.
19: The blood avenger shall himself put the murderer to death — when he meets him, he shall put him to death. The blood avenger acts as the community's agent of retributive justice, not as a private vigilante. His role is legitimate and expected within the legal structure.
20: If the killing was from enmity — if someone pushed another from hatred, or threw something at them deliberately, and they died — it is murder. Intent revealed through prior relationship and manner of attack determines guilt. The law sees through physical act to motive.
21: Striking from hostility so that death results — the striker is a murderer and shall be put to death by the blood avenger when he meets him. No city of refuge protects the intentional killer. The refuge is for the innocent-in-intent, not the guilty.
22: 'But if someone pushed another suddenly without enmity, or threw anything at them unintentionally...' — the shift to accidental killing begins with relational and circumstantial evidence. No prior hostility, no malicious forethought — the act was sudden and unforeseen.
23: ...or if without seeing someone a stone was dropped on them that could kill, and they died — and the person was not their enemy and did not seek their harm — then the congregation shall judge. Community discernment determines the boundary between murder and manslaughter. No individual makes this determination alone.
24: The congregation shall judge between the manslayer and the blood avenger according to these regulations. The congregation serves as tribunal — not the avenger, not the accused. The community's collective judgment protects against both unchecked revenge and unchecked impunity.
25: The congregation shall rescue the manslayer from the blood avenger and return him to the city of refuge. He shall live there until the death of the high priest (kohen hagadol) who was anointed with holy oil. This is the pivot of the chapter: the high priest's death is the mechanism of the manslayer's release.
26: If the manslayer goes outside the limits of the city of refuge and the blood avenger finds and kills him — no blood guilt attaches to the avenger. The protection is conditional on remaining within the city. Outside its boundaries, the manslayer is exposed. The refuge only protects those who stay within it.
27: For the manslayer must remain in his city of refuge until the death of the high priest; after the high priest's death he may return to his ancestral land. The high priest's death is the only lawful exit. This is a remarkable provision: the manslayer's freedom is bound to the life of the high priest. When the high priest dies, the manslayer goes free.
28: These shall be your statutes (chukat mishpat) for all your generations throughout all your settlements. The cities of refuge institution is permanent law, not a temporary arrangement for the wilderness period.
29: Whoever kills a person — the murderer shall be put to death on the testimony of witnesses (edim). But no one shall be put to death on the testimony of only one witness. Two witnesses minimum for a capital verdict — protecting against false accusation and personal revenge framed as justice.
30: You shall not accept a ransom (kofer) for the life of a murderer who is guilty of death; he shall be put to death. Kofer (ransom/substitute payment) is explicitly prohibited in murder cases. Life cannot be ransomed with money — only blood answers for blood. The prohibition reinforces the weight of human life made in God's image.
31: And you shall not accept a ransom for one who has fled to a city of refuge, allowing them to return to live in the land before the death of the high priest. The two prohibitions together — no ransom for murderers (v30), no ransom to shorten the manslayer's exile (v31) — seal the system. Money cannot buy its way around either judgment or mercy's timeline.
32: 'You shall not pollute the land in which you live, for blood pollutes the land, and no atonement (kapparah) can be made for the land for the blood shed in it, except by the blood of the one who shed it.' The land itself is a moral entity — it can be defiled (tamei) and it can receive atonement (kapparah). The same vocabulary used for ritual purity is applied to civic justice.
33: You shall not defile the land where you live and in the midst of which I dwell, for I, YHWH, dwell in the midst of the Israelites. The ultimate reason for these careful laws is YHWH's presence: ani YHWH shokhen betoch — I YHWH dwell in the midst. Where God dwells, the land must be kept clean. Justice is not merely civic — it is theological. A defiled land cannot hold a holy God.
34: The chapter closes returning to the theological center: the land belongs to YHWH; Israel merely sojourns in it. The cities of refuge, the witnesses requirement, the prohibition on ransom — all derive from this reality. Justice in the land reflects the character of the one who inhabits it.
